justification: All 6 rules classified correctly: BR-1 (true), BR-2 (false), BR-3 (false), BR-4 (true), BR-5 (false), BR-6 (true). Each has detailed written justification distinguishing state integrity from configurable policy. Rules 2, 3, 5 explicitly use 'tunable'/'configurable' reasoning. Grace period ambiguity surfaced in edge cases (Premium downgrade during grace period, day-14 boundary). BR-2 justification notes it should be configurable ('a VP could change this to 5 or 10 days'). The configurable-vs-invariant distinction is systematically applied throughout.

observations.text: All 6 invariant classifications are correct with no errors
Justifications are thorough and consistently distinguish 'state integrity constraint' from 'business policy tunable'
Grace period ambiguity surfaced in multiple edge cases: day-14 boundary, tier downgrade during grace period, and whether grace period replaces or supplements 3-strike rule
Configurable policy recommendation implicit in justifications (e.g., 'Management could decide to allow 5 retries or 2 retries')
Goes beyond requirements with email normalization edge cases and payment method timing edge cases
No explicit recommendation section saying 'store rules 2, 3, 5 as configuration not hard-coded constants' — the reasoning is there but not consolidated as a recommendation

trials.justification: All 6 rules classified correctly: BR-1 (invariant: true), BR-2 (invariant: false), BR-3 (invariant: false), BR-4 (invariant: true), BR-5 (invariant: false), BR-6 (invariant: true). Each rule has detailed written justification distinguishing state integrity constraints from configurable policy thresholds. Rules 2, 3, 5 explicitly use 'configurable' reasoning (e.g., 'a VP could change this', 'tolerance knob, not a consistency guard'). Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in edge cases (Premium-to-Standard downgrade mid-grace-period) and in low_confidence_areas ('Whether Premium grace period runs concurrently with or replaces retry attempts is unclear'). Configuration recommendation is implicit in the invariant:false justifications stating these are adjustable parameters.

observations.text: Perfect 6/6 invariant classifications with the correct true/false assignments
Justifications are exceptionally detailed, consistently using the 'state integrity vs configurable policy' framework
Grace period ambiguity surfaced in multiple places: edge cases and low confidence areas
Additional valuable edge cases identified beyond the rubric requirements (email aliasing, race conditions, renewal window overlap with cancellation)
No explicit recommendation to store rules 2, 3, 5 as configuration rather than hard-coded constants — the reasoning implies it but doesn't state it as an actionable recommendation
The specific ambiguity about what happens after the 14-day Premium grace period expires is captured in edge case 'Premium subscriber grace period expires on same day as successful retry'

Rules 2,3,5 explicitly use 'configurable' reasoning (e.g., 'policy knob', 'business could adjust'). Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in edge_cases (Premium subscriber downgrade during grace period) and in low_confidence_areas. observations.text: Perfect 6/6 invariant classification with no errors
Justifications clearly distinguish 'state integrity constraint' from 'configurable business policy' for every rule
Grace period ambiguity surfaced: 'does the grace period persist or does the 3-strike rule take over' when Premium downgrades during grace
